summary:
Amazon.com was founded by Jeff Bezos in July 1994 in Bellevue, Washington, initially as an online bookstore that he launched from his garage. Bezos recognized that books were a commodity product with a vast catalogue that translated particularly well to online retail — no physical store could stock the millions of titles that an online warehouse could offer. The company launched its website in 1995 and went public in 1997, raising $54 million in an IPO despite having never turned a profit.

Amazon rapidly expanded beyond books into music, DVDs, and then virtually every consumer product category, building a logistics and distribution infrastructure that redefined retail expectations for delivery speed and convenience. The company survived the [[event:3377|dot-com bubble]] collapse that destroyed most of its competitors, partly because Bezos maintained focus on long-term customer infrastructure over short-term profitability. Amazon's growth exemplified the disruptive potential of e-commerce for traditional retail and its development of Amazon Web Services would later make it a dominant force in cloud computing as well. By 2000, Amazon had become the defining example of internet commerce transformation and one of the most recognizable brands on the Web.
